channel.title: Political Science, History, and Statistics on André Walter
channel.description: Recent content in Political Science, History, and Statistics on André Walter

item.title: New Publication Forthcoming in JoESP
item.description:
My article, “Taking the Initiative. Direct Democracy, Coalition Governments, and Welfare State Expansion”, has been accepted at the Journal of Eruopean Social Policy. Below is the abstract:

Direct democratic rights are increasingly adopted around the world. However, what are the consequences of the introduction of initiative and referendum rights for welfare state development? The existing literature holds a pessimistic view on the role of direct democracy for welfare state development and generosity. Whereas referendums are argued to constitute a major road block to the extension of social expenditure, the link between initiatives and social security provision is unclear. I contribute to the existing literature by arguing that the effect of direct democratic instruments on welfare state extension is systematically related to the political environment. More precisely, I contend that governments with few parties deviate from the median voter preference by providing insufficient social expenditure whereas the opposite is true in settings with many governing parties. In both cases, voters employ direct legislation to correct for the spending behavior of governments. Using time-series cross-sectional data on social expenditure in the Swiss cantons from 1930 to 2000, I show that initiatives and referendums constrain social expenditure growth in settings with many governing parties but expand it when few parties are in government.

item.title: New Publication Forthcoming in JoP
item.description:
Another article, “A Race to the Middle. The Politics of Interstate Cost Distribution and Welfare State Expansion”, has been accepted at the Journal of Politics. Below is the abstract:

Previous research on welfare state development in federal states has claimed that interstate competition induces a race to the bottom with regard to social expenditure. While existing research has focused on the impact of migration inflows of poor households on states with high provision of social expenditure, it has remained silent with regard to migration outflows. In this paper, I argue that migration outflows are associated with increased social expenditure if eligibility restrictions for social benefits between states are relaxed. Facing outflows of workers and the unemployed, low-spending states respond with increases in social expenditure to retain a sufficient work force and counteract rising wage levels. As a result, states with higher levels of outward migration are catching up with more generous states instead of engaging in a race to the bottom. To support my claim, I use a time-series cross-section dataset of Swiss cantons covering the period 1930-1975.

item.title: CV
item.description:
Academic Employment

  • 2018– Postdoctoral Researcher, Chair for Comparative Political Economy and Public Policy, Department of Political Science, University of St. Gallen, Switzerland.
  • 2012–2018 Research Assistant, Chair for Comparative Political Economy and Public Policy, Department of Political Science, University of St. Gallen, Switzerland.
  • 2011–2012 Teaching Assistant, Gender Studies, University of Zurich, Switzerland.
  • 2009–2010 Student and Teaching Assistant, Institute of Sociology, University of Jena, Germany.

Education

  • 2018 PhD, Political Science, University of St. Gallen, Switzerland
  • 2012 MA, Sociology, Social-/Cultural Anthropology, and Gender Studies, University of Zurich, Switzerland
  • 2010 BA, Sociology and Political Science, University of Jena, Germany

Additional Training

2017

  • Machine Learning with R, Summer School in Empirical Research Methods, St. Gallen, Switzerland; Instructor: Brett Lantz
  • Bayesian Data Analysis, Summer School in Empirical Research Methods,St. Gallen, Switzerland; Instructor: Michael Kalish

2016

  • GIS for Armed Conflict Research, St. Gallen, Switzerland; Instructor: Jan Ketil Rod
  • Spatial Analysis for the Social Sciences, St. Gallen, Switzerland; Instructor: Michael D. Ward

2015

  • Categorical Data Analysis, Summer School in Empirical Research Methods, St. Gallen, Switzerland; Instructor: Shawna Smith
  • Maximum Likelihood Methods, Summer School in Empirical Research Methods, St. Gallen, Switzerland; Instructor: Christopher Zorn
  • Computational Statistics, Summer School in Empirical Research Methods, St. Gallen, Switzerland; Instructor: Francesco Audrino.
  • Panel Data Analysis, ECPR Winter School in Methods and Techniques, Bamberg, Germany; Instructor: Christian Assmann

2014

  • Regression II+III, Summer School in Empirical Research Methods, St. Gallen, Switzerland; Instructor: Brain M. Pollins

2013

  • Process Tracing Methodology I+II, ECPR Summer School in Methods and Techniques, Ljubljana, Slovenia; Instructor: Derek Beach, Rasmus Brun Pedersen
  • Case Study Research Methods, University of St. Gallen, Switzerland; Instructor: Andrew Bennett

Software

Reports

  • LaTeX, Word, Power Point, Excel

Statistics

  • R, Stata (SPSS)

GIS Data

  • QGIS, ARCGIS

item.title: Publications
item.description:
Published (*peer-reviewed)

  • (*)A Race to the Middle. The Politics of Interstate Cost Distribution and Welfare State Expansion. The Journal of Politics, forthcoming
  • (*)Taking the Initiative! Direct Democracy, Coalition Governments, and Welfare State Expansion, Journal of European Social Policy., forthcoming
  • (*)Measuring the Impacts of Colonialism: A New Data Set for the Countries of Africa and Asia. Journal of World-Systems Research, 23(1), 2017, 156-190 (with Patrick Ziltener and Daniel Kuenzler)

Other Publications (*peer-reviewed)

  • (*)Europäische Integration auf Unternehmensebene: Die Schweizer Beteiligung an Europäischen Betriebsräten (EBR). Swiss Journal of Sociology, 42(1), 2016, p. 159–174 (with Patrick Ziltener and Heinz Gabathuler)
  • (*)QCA, the Truth Table Analysis and Large-N Survey Data: The Benefits of Calibration and the Importance of Robustness Tests. 2014-79. (with Patrick Emmenegger and Dominik Schraff)

Unter Review

  • A Disproportional Threat. The Adoption of Proportional Representation in Switzerland (with Patrick Emmenegger), R&R
  • Swiss Cantonal Governments 1848-2017. A new Dataset. (with Patrick Emmenegger)
  • Majority Protection: The Origins of Distorted Proportional Representation (with Patrick Emmenegger)
  • The Social Origins of Christian Democracy. Rural-Urban Migration and the Rise of the Catholic Workers’ Movement

Working Papers

  • A Socialist Threat? Radical Party Entry and the Formation of Electoral Alliances in Majoritarian Systems
  • Interest Mediation or Front Line? Socialist Electoral Mobilization and the Growth of the Catholic Workers’ Movement
  • Intra-Elite Competition and the Origins of Direct Taxation (with Patrick Emmenegger and Lucas Leemann)
  • Beyond the Neolithic Revolution. Endogenous Political Development, External Domination and the Formation of Pre-Colonial and Colonial States.
  • When Dominant Parties Adopt PR. The Mysterious Case of Belgium (with Patrick Emmenegger).

Work in Progress

  • Initiating Welfare Competition. How the Freedom of Movement for the Poor was introduced (with Cecilia Bruzelius)
  • The Adoption of Personal Income Taxation in the Swiss Cantons [Working Title] (with Patrick Emmenegger and Lucas Leemann)
  • How Socialism Changed Politics. Large-Scale Evidence from District Competition and Parliamentary Debates in Imperial Germany using Quantitative Text Analysis
  • Electoral System Change, Political Competition and Targeted Elite Mobilization
  • Persisting Inequality. The Structure of the World Income Distribution 1975-2016 (with Patrick Ziltener)
  • The Abolition of Feudal Taxation across Europe [Working Title]
  • Wasted Votes? Electoral Competition, Vote Defection, and Strategic Third Party Entry in Single Member Districts
  • The Black Coalition. Why the German Reich Adopted Social Insurance
